why.title: Independence in Vlore, 1912
why.text: Albania marks the declaration of independence from the Ottoman Empire on 28 November 1912. The same date is also linked to Skanderbeg raising the Albanian flag in 1443.

anthem.title: Himni i Flamurit
anthem.note: The anthem is centered on the flag as a symbol of unity, sacrifice and national awakening.
anthem.created: 1908 poem; melody from 1880s Romanian patriotic song

why.title: Patron saint of Andorra
why.text: Andorra marks 8 September as the feast of Our Lady of Meritxell, the country's patron saint. The shrine at Meritxell is closely tied to Andorran identity.

anthem.title: El Gran Carlemany
anthem.note: The anthem presents Andorra as a small Pyrenean nation protected by history, faith and Charlemagne legend.

why.title: Republic restored in 1991
why.text: Armenia celebrates the 21 September 1991 referendum that confirmed independence from the Soviet Union. The day is about statehood, survival and the modern Armenian republic.

anthem.title: Mer Hayrenik
anthem.note: The anthem speaks about homeland, freedom and sacrifice, using a song connected to the First Republic.

why.title: Neutrality and modern civic identity
why.text: Austria National Day is observed on 26 October because it marks neutrality and modern civic identity. The local programme changes by city, but the national meaning of the date stays the same.

anthem.title: Land der Berge, Land am Strome
anthem.note: The anthem describes Austria through mountains, rivers, work, culture and a hopeful republic.
anthem.composer: Melody traditionally attributed to Mozart or Johann Holzer

why.title: First democratic republic, 1918
why.text: Azerbaijan marks the proclamation of the Azerbaijan Democratic Republic on 28 May 1918, remembered as the first secular parliamentary republic in the Muslim world.

anthem.title: Azerbaijan March
anthem.note: The anthem is a march about the homeland, liberty and readiness to defend Azerbaijan.

why.title: Liberation of Minsk in 1944
why.text: Belarus marks Independence Day on 3 July, the date Minsk was liberated from Nazi occupation in 1944. The holiday links statehood to wartime memory.

anthem.title: My Belarusy
anthem.note: The anthem emphasizes homeland, unity, peace and continuity after wartime suffering.
anthem.writer: Michael Klimkovich; revised lyrics by Uladzimir Karyzna

why.title: Leopold I takes the oath
why.text: Belgium celebrates 21 July 1831, when Leopold I swore allegiance to the constitution as the first King of the Belgians after independence.

anthem.title: La Brabanconne
anthem.note: The anthem grew out of the Belgian Revolution and calls for liberty, law and national loyalty.
anthem.writer: Jenneval; later revised by Charles Rogier

why.title: ZAVNOBiH statehood, 1943
why.text: Statehood Day marks the 25 November 1943 ZAVNOBiH declaration that Bosnia and Herzegovina was shared by its peoples, not the possession of one group.

anthem.title: National Anthem of Bosnia and Herzegovina
anthem.note: The anthem is instrumental in official use, which avoids choosing one language text for the state.

why.title: Liberation from Ottoman rule
why.text: Bulgaria marks 3 March 1878, the Treaty of San Stefano date after the Russo-Turkish War, as a symbol of liberation and restored Bulgarian statehood.

anthem.title: Mila Rodino
anthem.note: The anthem praises the homeland's mountains, rivers and beauty, with a patriotic refrain.

why.title: Modern parliament and sovereignty
why.text: Croatia marks 30 May 1990, when the first democratically elected multi-party parliament was constituted, connecting the holiday to modern democratic statehood.

anthem.title: Lijepa nasa domovino
anthem.note: The anthem is a landscape hymn to the homeland, rivers, fields, sea and loyalty.

why.title: Independence from Britain, 1960
why.text: Cyprus celebrates the founding of the Republic of Cyprus after British rule. The public holiday is observed on 1 October.

anthem.title: Hymn to Liberty
anthem.note: Cyprus uses the Greek Hymn to Liberty, a poem about freedom after struggle.

why.title: Czechoslovak independence, 1918
why.text: Czechia marks 28 October 1918, when independent Czechoslovakia was proclaimed after Austria-Hungary collapsed.

anthem.title: Kde domov muj
anthem.note: The anthem asks where home is and answers with Czech landscapes, water, gardens and peace.

why.title: Constitution of 1849
why.text: Denmark marks the 5 June 1849 constitution, which ended absolute monarchy and introduced constitutional government. The date also carries later constitutional reforms.

anthem.title: Der er et yndigt land
anthem.note: The anthem is a calm patriotic song about Denmark's landscape, old history and enduring people.

why.title: Declaration of independence, 1918
why.text: Estonia celebrates 24 February 1918, when the Manifesto to the Peoples of Estonia declared independence. The date later symbolized restored sovereignty after occupation.

anthem.title: Mu isamaa, mu onn ja room
anthem.note: The anthem is a prayer-like song for homeland, happiness and blessing.

why.title: Independence declared in 1917
why.text: Finland celebrates 6 December 1917, when parliament adopted the declaration of independence from Russia. The day is solemn, candle-lit and strongly tied to national resilience.

anthem.title: Maamme / Vart land
anthem.note: The anthem praises the homeland's nature and the people's love for a modest northern country.

why.title: Revolution and republic
why.text: France's national day recalls the storming of the Bastille on 14 July 1789 and the Fete de la Federation in 1790. It links revolution, citizenship and republican unity.

anthem.title: La Marseillaise
anthem.note: The anthem is a revolutionary war song calling citizens to defend liberty.

why.title: First republic declared in 1918
why.text: Georgia marks 26 May 1918, when the Democratic Republic of Georgia declared independence after the Russian Empire collapsed.

anthem.title: Tavisupleba
anthem.note: The title means Freedom; the anthem links liberty, homeland and Georgian cultural memory.
anthem.composer: Zakaria Paliashvili themes arranged by Ioseb Kechakmadze

why.title: Reunification in 1990
why.text: Germany celebrates 3 October 1990, when East and West Germany were formally reunified. The day is about democracy, unity and the end of Cold War division.

anthem.title: Deutschlandlied
anthem.note: Official use focuses on the third stanza: unity, justice and freedom.

why.title: War of Independence begins
why.text: Greece marks 25 March 1821 as the traditional start of the War of Independence from Ottoman rule. The date also coincides with the Annunciation in the Orthodox calendar.

anthem.title: Hymn to Liberty
anthem.note: The anthem is drawn from a long poem identifying liberty through struggle and sacrifice.

why.title: Foundation of the Hungarian state
why.text: Hungary celebrates Saint Stephen, its first king, on 20 August. The day links Christian state foundation, monarchy and modern national identity.

anthem.title: Himnusz
anthem.note: The anthem is a prayer for the Hungarian people, recalling hardship, history and hope.

why.title: Republic founded in 1944
why.text: Iceland celebrates 17 June 1944, when it became a republic and ended the union with Denmark. The date is also Jon Sigurdsson's birthday.

anthem.title: Lofsongur
anthem.note: The anthem is a hymn-like song with religious language and praise for Iceland.

why.title: Saint Patrick and Irish culture
why.text: Saint Patrick's Day is observed on 17 March because it marks Saint Patrick and Irish culture. The local programme changes by city, but the national meaning of the date stays the same.

anthem.title: Amhran na bhFiann
anthem.note: The anthem is a soldiers' song from the independence era, focused on freedom and commitment.

why.title: Republic referendum, 1946
why.text: Italy celebrates 2 June 1946, when voters chose a republic over monarchy after World War II. It is the birthday of the modern Italian Republic.

anthem.title: Il Canto degli Italiani
anthem.note: Known as Fratelli d'Italia, it calls Italians to unity and national revival.

why.title: Independence declared in 2008
why.text: Kosovo marks 17 February 2008, when its parliament declared independence. The day is about modern statehood and international recognition.

anthem.title: Europe
anthem.note: Kosovo chose an instrumental anthem to avoid privileging one language or community.

why.title: Republic proclaimed in 1918
why.text: Latvia celebrates 18 November 1918, when the Republic of Latvia was proclaimed in Riga, later becoming a symbol of restored sovereignty.

anthem.title: Dievs, sveti Latviju!
anthem.note: The title means God Bless Latvia; the anthem asks blessing for the Latvian people and homeland.

why.title: Prince and Assumption feast
why.text: Liechtenstein celebrates 15 August, combining the Feast of the Assumption with the princely birthday tradition and a public monarchy-centred national day.

anthem.title: Oben am jungen Rhein
anthem.note: The anthem praises the young Rhine, the homeland and loyalty to Liechtenstein.
anthem.composer: Melody of God Save the King

why.title: Act of Independence, 1918
why.text: Lithuania marks 16 February 1918, when the Council of Lithuania signed the Act of Independence, the central date of modern Lithuanian statehood.

anthem.title: Tautiska giesme
anthem.note: The anthem calls Lithuania a land of heroes and asks citizens to draw strength from the past.

why.title: Grand Duke's official birthday
why.text: Luxembourg's National Day is the official celebration of the Grand Duke's birthday, fixed on 23 June as a monarchy and state celebration.

anthem.title: Ons Heemecht
anthem.note: The anthem praises Luxembourg as the homeland, its rivers and peaceful identity.

why.title: Last British forces leave, 1979
why.text: Malta marks 31 March 1979, when the last British troops left the island, making the day about full sovereignty after independence.

anthem.title: L-Innu Malti
anthem.note: The anthem is a prayer for Malta, asking protection, wisdom and unity.

why.title: Independence from the Soviet Union, 1991
why.text: Moldova celebrates 27 August 1991, when parliament adopted the declaration of independence from the Soviet Union.

anthem.title: Limba noastra
anthem.note: The anthem celebrates the Romanian language as a vessel of identity, memory and dignity.

why.title: Prince's Day
why.text: Monaco's National Day is the Sovereign Prince's Day, currently observed on 19 November as a dynastic and civic celebration of the principality.

anthem.title: Hymne Monegasque
anthem.note: The anthem praises Monaco, its prince and the principality's identity.
anthem.writer: Louis Notari revised Monegasque text; earlier French text by Theophile Bellando de Castro

why.title: Berlin Congress recognition, 1878
why.text: Montenegro marks 13 July 1878, when the Congress of Berlin recognized it as independent. The date also recalls the 1941 uprising.

anthem.title: Oj, svijetla majska zoro
anthem.note: The anthem invokes Montenegro's bright dawn, mountains, honour and freedom.
anthem.writer: Traditional text revised in modern form

why.title: Monarch's birthday celebration
why.text: King's Day celebrates the Dutch monarch's birthday, currently King Willem-Alexander on 27 April, and has become the country's largest orange public celebration.

anthem.title: Wilhelmus
anthem.note: The anthem is voiced as William of Orange and reflects loyalty, revolt and faith during the Dutch struggle for independence.
anthem.writer: Attributed to Philips van Marnix van Sint-Aldegonde

why.title: Independence referendum, 1991
why.text: North Macedonia celebrates 8 September 1991, when voters backed independence from Yugoslavia.

anthem.title: Denes nad Makedonija
anthem.note: The anthem celebrates a new sun of freedom and the struggle for Macedonian statehood.

title: Norwegian Constitution Day
happens: Children parades, flags and local ceremonies
happensNote: The national date is fixed; the route, timing and crowd pattern are decided locally.

anthem.title: Ja, vi elsker dette landet
anthem.note: Words by Bjornstjerne Bjornson, melody by Rikard Nordraak. Often sung at school and civic gatherings on 17 May.

why.title: Constitution signed at Eidsvoll, 1814
why.text: Norway celebrates 17 May 1814, when the constitution was signed at Eidsvoll. The day became strongly associated with children's parades, flags, civic pride and independence from union rule.

why.title: Independence restored in 1918
why.text: Poland celebrates 11 November 1918, when independence was restored after partitions by neighbouring empires. The date also aligns with the end of World War I.

anthem.title: Mazurek Dabrowskiego
anthem.note: The anthem promises that Poland has not perished while its people live and fight for freedom.

why.title: Camoes and Portuguese culture
why.text: Portugal Day marks 10 June, the death date of poet Luis de Camoes in 1580. It celebrates Portugal, Portuguese communities and the Portuguese language.

anthem.title: A Portuguesa
anthem.note: The anthem began as a patriotic response to crisis and calls Portugal to rise for the nation.

why.title: Union of Transylvania with Romania, 1918
why.text: Romania celebrates 1 December 1918, when the Great National Assembly at Alba Iulia voted union of Transylvania with Romania.

anthem.title: Desteapta-te, romane!
anthem.note: The anthem is a revolutionary call to awaken, defend liberty and remember national dignity.

why.title: The 1990 declaration of state sovereignty
why.text: Russia Day is observed on 12 June because it marks the 1990 declaration of state sovereignty. The local programme changes by city, but the national meaning of the date stays the same.

anthem.title: State Anthem of the Russian Federation
anthem.note: The anthem uses Soviet-era music with new lyrics about unity, homeland and a vast state.

why.title: Traditional founding in 301
why.text: San Marino celebrates 3 September as the feast of Saint Marinus and the traditional founding of the republic in 301.

anthem.title: Inno Nazionale della Repubblica
anthem.note: The anthem is normally instrumental, matching San Marino's formal civic ceremonies.
anthem.writer: No official lyrics in ordinary use

why.title: Uprising and constitution
why.text: Serbia celebrates 15 February, Sretenje, linking the First Serbian Uprising in 1804 and the 1835 Sretenje Constitution.

anthem.title: Boze pravde
anthem.note: The anthem is a prayer for justice, protection and the Serbian homeland.

why.title: Constitution adopted in 1992
why.text: Slovakia marks 1 September 1992, when the Slovak constitution was adopted before the peaceful split of Czechoslovakia.

anthem.title: Nad Tatrou sa blyska
anthem.note: The anthem pictures lightning over the Tatras and a people awakening after storm.

why.title: Independence declared in 1991
why.text: Slovenia celebrates 25 June 1991, when it declared independence from Yugoslavia and began modern Slovenian statehood.

anthem.title: Zdravljica
anthem.note: Slovenia uses the poem's seventh stanza, a toast to peace, neighbours and free nations.

why.title: 12 October and Hispanic heritage
why.text: Spain marks 12 October, traditionally linked to Columbus's 1492 arrival in the Americas and now framed as Spain's national day. It combines state ceremony, military parade and debates about history.

anthem.title: Marcha Real
anthem.adopted: Officially used from the 18th century; restored 1978
anthem.note: Spain's anthem is one of the few national anthems without official lyrics.

why.title: Gustav Vasa and the 1809 constitution
why.text: Sweden celebrates 6 June because Gustav Vasa was elected king on 6 June 1523, often treated as the start of modern Sweden, and because the 1809 Instrument of Government was adopted on the same date.

anthem.title: Du gamla, du fria
anthem.adopted: De facto anthem; not formally adopted by law
anthem.note: The song is not a battle hymn. It is about the old, free Nordic homeland, its landscape, memory and quiet love of country.

why.title: Federal Charter tradition, 1291
why.text: Switzerland celebrates 1 August, linked to the traditional 1291 Federal Charter between early Alpine communities. The day is about confederation, local autonomy and Swiss civic identity.

anthem.title: Swiss Psalm
anthem.note: The anthem is a prayer-like song connecting Swiss landscapes with gratitude and freedom.

title: Republic Day of Turkey
reason: the proclamation of the Republic in 1923

why.title: The proclamation of the Republic in 1923
why.text: Republic Day of Turkey is observed on 29 October because it marks the proclamation of the Republic in 1923. The local programme changes by city, but the national meaning of the date stays the same.

anthem.title: Istiklal Marsi
anthem.note: The anthem is a passionate independence march about flag, faith, sacrifice and freedom.

why.title: Independence declared in 1991
why.text: Ukraine celebrates 24 August 1991, when parliament adopted the Act of Declaration of Independence. The date became even more charged after Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.

anthem.title: Shche ne vmerla Ukrainy i slava, i volia
anthem.note: The anthem says Ukraine's glory and freedom have not perished and promises continued struggle for liberty.

title: Chair of Saint Peter
reason: feast of the Chair of Saint Peter

why.title: Feast of the Chair of Saint Peter
why.text: Vatican City uses 22 February, the Feast of the Chair of Saint Peter, as its national day because the state exists as the seat of the papacy.

anthem.title: Inno e Marcia Pontificale
anthem.note: The anthem honours the pope and the Holy See, functioning as the Vatican's ceremonial anthem.
anthem.writer: Antonio Allegra wrote the Italian text; Raffaello Lavagna later wrote Latin text

places.name: Brasilia
places.note: official capital programme and civic ceremonies

places.name: Rio de Janeiro
places.note: large-city holiday crowds and public spaces

places.name: Sao Paulo
places.note: urban holiday base with transport planning needed

why.title: Independence from Portugal, 1822
why.text: Brazil celebrates 7 September 1822, when Dom Pedro declared independence from Portugal near the Ipiranga River. The day is the central civic date of Brazilian statehood.

anthem.title: Hino Nacional Brasileiro
anthem.note: The anthem uses poetic language about liberty, the homeland, nature and the promise of Brazil.

places.name: Santiago
places.note: largest urban base and civic holiday programme

places.name: Fondas
places.note: traditional food, music and dancing venues

why.title: First Government Junta, 1810
why.text: Chile's Fiestas Patrias centre on 18 September 1810, when the First Government Junta began the independence process. The holiday also spills into food, dance and family gatherings.

anthem.title: Himno Nacional de Chile
anthem.note: The anthem praises Chile's landscape, freedom and civic duty.

places.name: Nadi
places.note: visitor base; check local holiday services

places.name: Resort areas
places.note: ask locally which ceremonies or cultural moments are open

why.title: Independence in 1970
why.text: Fiji Day marks 10 October 1970, when Fiji became independent from British rule. The date also echoes the 1874 cession date, giving it layered colonial and independence meaning.

anthem.title: God Bless Fiji / Meda Dau Doka
anthem.note: The anthem asks for blessing and unity, with English and Fijian versions used in national life.
